title: Reducing paper waste in the home
subtitle: Stopping junk mail, switching to digital billing, and reducing printing
excerpt: The average UK household receives around 70 kg of paper per year, a quarter of which is unsolicited. Stopping junk mail, switching bills and statements to digital, and reducing unnecessary printing can cut the paper footprint by 30-50 percent without any inconvenience.
sourceNotes: Royal Mail junk mail opt-out procedures. Mailing Preference Service registration documentation. WRAP household paper consumption dataset (2022). Carbon Trust paper lifecycle analysis.

content.text: Paper accounts for approximately 20-25 percent of household waste by weight in the UK. Most of it is packaging and mail. The portion of this that is directly controllable at household level includes addressed advertising mail, unaddressed leaflets, printed bills and statements, and self-generated printing. Each has a different opt-out or reduction route.

Register with the Mailing Preference Service (mpsonline.org.uk). This suppresses your address from the databases used by UK direct mail companies. It takes two to three months to take effect and is free. It does not stop mail from organisations you have a prior relationship with; to stop those, contact each organisation directly and request removal.
Fill in the Royal Mail opt-out form on their website to stop their door-to-door leaflet service. A No junk mail sign works with most leaflet companies but is not binding on all of them.

Switching to paperless billing with each utility, bank, and insurer eliminates regular statements. Most providers offer this option in account settings. For records that must be kept, downloading PDF statements is no slower than filing paper copies and removes the physical storage.
Set the default printer setting to double-sided (duplex). Most printers support duplex printing; it halves the paper use for any multi-page document.
Check page count before printing. Web pages often print to many more pages than visible on screen due to navigation bars and footers. Print to PDF to preview before committing to paper.
Use digital boarding passes, tickets, and receipts wherever offered. These are accepted at all UK airports and most venues.

Cardboard, newspapers, and magazines can go in the kerbside recycling bin, torn into strips for the compost heap, or laid as mulch. Coated or lined paper (coffee cups, juice cartons, till receipts) cannot go in the recycling bin and goes in general waste.
